focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By November the library windows had gone black by half past four, and the blown-out north bay held the evening cold like a cistern. Owen sat on the floor between two displaced cases with Binns's old *History* across his knees and his ash-linen notebook open beside one shoe. He had come up there because the damage still made a pocket no prefect bothered with, a wedge of cracked shelving and dust where the draught reached first and people reached last. Also because his wand had turned sulky after the greenhouse business, and writing with a pen had lately been less humiliating than trusting a charm to do what he asked.

The burn across his right palm had learned the weather before the rest of him. It pulled tight whenever the stone went cold. Tonight it felt as if a thin hot wire had been stitched under the skin and was being drawn in small patient increments each time he flattened his hand to steady the page.

He had copied the same blank table three times and disliked all three copies. The original in the Grinlow Leaves was not properly blank anymore, not to him. It had pressure in it. The page answered his burned hand with the sense of ruled spaces and something almost written just beyond them, as if the text was standing in another room with the door not quite shut. On paper from the stationer it collapsed back into ordinary failure.

A shadow crossed the floorboards and then held still.

"Owen."

He looked up too fast and knocked his pen. Ink dotted the floor, his sock, the cuff of his robe. Prue Hollis stood over the gap between the shelves with three books braced against her chest and her scarf knotted badly enough to suggest she had done it while walking. Her spectacles had fogged at the edges from the change in temperature. She pushed them up with the...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...tuck.

"What?" Owen said.

"The edition." She pushed her spectacles up with one knuckle. "The footnote you quoted. `North Stacks C-13, sealed by founders' agreement.` Which printing had that? Because the classroom text drops the founder shelf marks before the 1883 clean issue, and after the 1894 recatalogue nobody writes `C-13` for anything in the north stacks. So either you've had a breakdown in a very specific bibliography, or somebody cut that note out of every copy that ordinary people can still find."

For a moment Owen only looked at her. He had spent two days with a discovery so large it made the school year feel like a bad local arrangement of furniture. It had not occurred to him until then that the first person who might believe him would do it because the citation style was wrong.

"The title page was half gone," he said. His voice came back rough. "But it was old. Binns's classroom shelf. Not library stock."

The girl's gaze sharpened into the grim concentration of someone seeing a filing error big enough to imply deliberate damage.

"Right," she said. "I thought so."

"Prue," Martha called from across the room, distracted and irritated. "If you're done courting mildew, I need the dry bandages."

The girl did not look away from Owen. "In a minute."

Then, quieter, to him: "Don't wave that about in here again."

Her eyes flicked to the bundle under his arm, then to Neville's map and the camp beds. Before he could answer, she crossed the room to Martha and took the dry bandages out of her hands.

Owen stayed by the latch a moment longer, feeling the burn in his palm pulse through the handkerchief.

By the time he left the room, the question pressing hardest was no longer how to explain the pages to anybody else. It was what, exactly, they wanted from his hand.
